verdict_reasoning: EREG (Epiregulin) is an EGF-family growth factor encoded as a single-pass type I transmembrane proligand. The protein is synthesized as a ~17 kDa TM-anchored precursor on the cell surface, where it can engage in juxtacrine ErbB1/ErbB4 signaling before being cleaved by sheddases (ADAM10/ADAM17) to release the ~6 kDa soluble mature form, which is the dominant circulating/secreted biological actor. The TM precursor stage is real and surface-accessible, supporting juxtacrine signaling, but is transient relative to the shed form. This matches the 'dual_localization / transient TM proligand' pattern: the canonical biological output is the soluble shed ectodomain, but the membrane-anchored precursor does transiently populate the outer leaflet. Expression is also relatively tissue-restricted (colon, stomach, placenta, inflammatory contexts), reinforcing contextual rather than ubiquitous surface display.
